sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Quite a lot to discuss at last month’s committee meeting.  We all agreed that the Fly-In combined with the X-Dales had been a great success.  The good weather certainly helped but, with about 100 attendees, it far exceeded our expectations.  When all the pennies had been counted up it became apparent that we had made a profit of just over £400.  The committee unanimously agreed that this money would be ‘ring fenced’ to be used as a float towards future Fly-Ins, when the weather might not be so kind.

The Club/Farmers’ Dinner on the Saturday night at Knight Stainforth was also successful, securing the goodwill of our generous landowners for another year.  However, the general consensus was that it rather distracted from Fly-In and the finish of the X-Dales, which were a couple of miles away at Langcliffe.

The original idea had been to invite the farmers to the Fly-In, treat them to a hog roast/BBQ and a few drinks, and entertain them with some ground handling races, spot landings and the competitors finishing the X-Dales.  But reality dawned, and we chickened out of that idea, mostly because of the unpredictable weather at that time of year (it has to be then to get the space on the campsite).  The risk of standing around in the rain, in a muddy field, with nothing to see, and subjecting the farmers to food poisoning was just too great to contemplate.  It’s the classic dilemma faced by all hang gliding and paragliding competition and event organisers in the UK: you need to commit to a date and book facilities without any guarantees about the weather.

We also discussed attracting more pilots to the dinner, but recognised that, whilst pilots are willing to travel long distances to fly, they are not so keen to do the same for a function, especially if they also need to book overnight accommodation.  In contrast, in the unlikely event of great flying weather, there is also the danger that pilots will go XC and not get back in time for the dinner.  I have a proposal to put to the committee that the members (mostly committee) who host the farmers will get their meal paid for by the club.

We decided that, in future, we’d keep the Farmers’ Dinner separate from the Fly-In.  Having already slipped the dinner from the Spring to the Autumn, it seemed a little unfair on the farmers to delay it by another 18 months in order to bring it back to the Spring again, so we intend to run the next one in the Autumn of 2024 (subject to the agreement of the farmers/sites officers).

That decision had the unintended consequence of pushing this significant expense into the following financial year.  That said, the savings might come in this FY because the Knights Table still hasn’t sent us a bill for the last dinner!  Either way the club has made a saving of about £1100 (on paper).

That saving, together with a small surplus from this year, led to a discussion about reducing subscriptions by £5 per member.  However, the majority felt that it would be sensible to leave things as they are, rather than having to raise them again in a year or so, once this one-off saving has evaporated.

 

In previous years, at the request of the farmers, we have presented our club trophies in front of them at the dinner.  We’ve decided to change that as well.  It seems more appropriate to present our prizes in front of club members.  To that end our prize giving will take place at the club night on 7 Dec, following the AGM.

sections.title: Social - First Club Night This Thursday!
sections.content:
Stefan Sykes Reports

This Winter Season sees a host of fine speakers, events and celebrations. They will all be held at the Horse and Farrier pub in Otley with a start time of 8pm, although most arrive beforehand to warm up a few pints or have a meal.

Please put the following dates in your diaries:

October 5th - Ed Cleasby and Stef Sykes will provide a debrief and discussion of the X Dales and Fly In 2023. Your feedback and ideas for 2024 all welcome.

November 2nd - Tom Shuttleworth will give a talk on his first 30 hours of flying. These are our most formative hours in which we’re learning the sites, weather apps, communication, equipment and ourselves.

December 7th - AGM and DHPC Annual Awards Ceremony. Please don’t forget to respond to the requests to engage with members' votes on some of these…

January 11th - Richard Meek regales us with the story of his Greatest Flight of 2023. No further details needed!

February 1st - DHPC Film and Photo Competition. Entries to social@dhpc.org.uk as soon as you like. Generous prizes…

March 7th - Kacper Kinstler reports on his exploits at the BP Cup in Bulgaria.

April 4th - Rhian Ebrey leads a discussion on how we bring more young people and women into the sport of free flying.

These bookings may change depending on circumstances.

A hearty thanks to Mike Barker, Librarian extraordinaire, who will be assisting with hosting some of these club nights.

I look forward to seeing you in the pub or on the hill.

sections.title: AGM Notice
sections.content:
DHPC Annual General Meeting

December 7th 2023. 7.30 for 8 pm start

Horse and Farrier, Bridge St, Otley. LS21 1BQ

All members of the Dales Hang Gliding and Paragliding Club are invited to attend the AGM, which will be followed by the presentation of the coveted club trophies and mugs for the 2023 season.

All proposals will be published in the November edition of Skywords and on the club forum. The Treasurer's report and reports from the other current committee members will be published on the forum by November 20th (members only). There is no opportunity to raise new issues for voting after 1st November, or on the night at the AGM. Voting on all proposals will be at the AGM in person only.

As well as setting membership subscriptions and contributions to the Flying Fund for the coming year, all committee posts are up for re-election, as per the constitution. Members are free to stand for any post - none of the incumbents will be offended if you wish to have a go at their role. Committee membership simply requires a little spare time and a willingness to put something back into the club. If you want to have a non-committal conversation about a role, then please contact the Chairman, Martin Baxter, at chairman@dhpc.org.uk.

Mark Tam Morrison

DHPC Secretary

sections.title: Dales Fly In
sections.content:
Stef Sykes reports

The second weekend in September - write it into your diary for 2024 now. If it’s going to be as good as 2023 then you won’t want to miss it.

Especially not for £11….

It was the weather that made it. After months of rain and wind, the jet stream went for a holiday leaving us some sun, light winds and warmth.

That was all we needed.

Tam, Adam and Stef set the site up on Wednesday when some people were already starting to arrive. By Thursday Tam was busy from 7am counting people in and by Saturday we were over 100 on site, to include two with paramotors.

Flying took place every day, using sites as varied as Dodd, Wether, Tailbridge, Windbank, Semer and others. The X Dales went off on the Saturday and took many as marshals off site.

We had Guy (Ginger Nomad) setting up his stall enjoying a brisk trade as well as Ed holding court at the X Dales Registration Desk in the main marquee.

The fire raged for three nights, but we still didn’t get through all the wood (thanks Simon) or all the beer - although that was a close thing.

Feedback from attendees who had come as far off as Dorset, South Wales and London was genuinely excellent. Many had never flown the Dales and were delighted with the hospitality, the support and the flying. Many even vowed to become Dales club members.

Thanks to all who contributed and to all who came along.

sections.title: Farmers Dinner
sections.content:
Adam Cox reports

The Dales Farmers’ Dinner is an age old tradition in the club and if you've never been, you should! A chance to have a proper feast with other flyers and have a chat with the farmers who make our fun possible!

I've mentioned the Farmers dinner to people in other clubs who always say what a great idea it is and that they don't have anything like that in their club. Most flying clubs pay farmers for sites which is one way of doing it but it's no more than a transaction. This is an event and a coming together - a real bond between two very different communities.

I think the farmers would be the first to admit “they don't get out much!” The life of a farmer doesn't include much leisure time, and the farmers themselves don't see each other socially much at all. The club is offering something much more valuable than money, a place for a hardworking, traditional community to do the two things they like best - have a good gossip and a great meal!

 

The evening started with Martin manning the minibus to bring the farmers in with the usual military precision we have come to expect. Chauffeured from Hawes to The Knights Table at Stainforth https://www.the-knights-table.co.uk.

The Knights Table is ideally suited for the occasion as it caters for large numbers very well and is very friendly with great grub. It's also a superb coincidence that the owner, Chris, is a paraglider pilot and member of the club! The atmosphere was great and everyone had a cracking time.

Over coffee Martin, our chairman, said a few words about the year's weather patterns which was attentively listened to by all. He thanked Tam and Stef who did all the organising to make the event the success it was.

This year was a first by moving the farmers’ dinner to September and tying in with the Fly-In and X-Dales competition to make a triple-whammy. This meant that the winners of the X-Dales received their awards from Ed Cleasby in front of a very appreciative audience who's land had been flown over.  Although there was some debate about the best time of year to stage the farmers’ dinner in future, it was decided that the idea of bringing all three events together had been a resounding success!

sections.title: X-Dales 23
sections.content:
Paul Rowntree reports

Year 3, new format.  Instead of a set course, 28 cylinders, with 20-150 points for tagging each one, were carefully placed by Ed Cleasby on hills largely around the Three Peaks.  Maps were studied and courses planned.  The forecast gave three days that would potentially work and Saturday was chosen.

Tom Shuttleworth and I chatted to Ed the day before.  By chance we had had the same strategy.  Ed’s feedback was fairly clear, “it looks ambitious, have you considered…”.  Tom and I went for a drive around the eastern part of the course.  What stood out was that the slopes that we thought were soarable weren’t that steep and the hills are a long way from each other.  Flying from one to the next without a thermal isn’t going to happen, a fair bit of walking will be required.

21 competitors made the starting line at Ribblehead.  The forecast was for light south westerly winds strengthening as the day progressed with little, if any, thermic activity, but the temperatures were in the 20’s and it was humid.  Once we’d crossed the line at 8:15, some carried straight on, some turned left, some right, others turned around.  Everyone’s different tactics had kicked in.  It suddenly turned competitive.

Only three of us went east.  Penyghent was my first target.  Walking along the Three Peaks trail against the flow of traffic, I achieved a pb for the number of times I’ve said “morning” in a day!  Stood at the top, I could see the tops of Ingleborough and Whernside in cloud (below photo from Penyghent towards Ingleborough), I’d lucked out where others hadn’t, I was able to take off, but with the wind being light, my hope of achieving a little bit of lift in flying around the hill wasn’t forthcoming.

 

Another climb to my next planned take off, to find a dry stone wall in my way with no gaps.  Option 1 walk down again, option 2 walk over the hill the other direction hoping to find a gap in the wall, or option 3 just take off from there with the wind 120 degrees off.  If you’ve never taken off with the wind blowing over the slope (a first for me), note the air is falling over the hill.  I had a steep grassy slope and at one stage was wondering if I’d be running to the bottom, but the feet eventually left the ground.  Option 2 may have been the best and then taking off from the other side of the hill, but I hadn’t considered going down the other valley would get me to a similar location at the time.

On landing, it started raining.  That wasn’t forecast.  I then bumped into Rosie Ireland walking the opposite direction, the rain had stopped her taking off from her first planned launch.  Of note, if I’d opted for option 2, by the time I’d got there, it would have been raining!  The point being that during the event numerous decisions are required, possibly changing the original strategy, which may be influenced by changing conditions and assessing the terrain, that most of us have not been to all of it before.

Local knowledge/good planning helps.  Later on I arrived at a tall dry stone wall with no gaps, I walked to the top of the hill, with no luck, then someway down the hill, it was nearly steep enough to take off and fly over the wall, then a gateway appeared.  Between me and the next waypoint was a sea of heather with no paths.  It looked good, but it was hard and slow walking through it.  Later that evening, I was assured there was a path along that ridge just on the other side of the hill.  The map on my phone didn’t show it, but the paper OS map did.  More planning required next time, or a bit of patience to stop and look at that map.

 

I’d aimed to be at Arncliffe by 2:30pm, which would give me plenty of time to walk back for the 6:30pm finish time, there was a 1 point penalty for every minute late.  I was an hour late at Arncliffe.  Some post-race analysis; on a stone/tarmac, not too steep track, I walk at c5.5 kph, across the heather, decent inclines and peat bog paths, c3.5 kph at best, up steep hills, very slowly!  A large part of the return trip was on tarmac, so at a strong march I was able to make up some time on the speed I guessed I’d move at before the race.  I resisted the temptation from some of the Fly-in pilots driving back to the campsite offering me a lift!  This is where the top competitors do a lot more planning.  They know the speed they run and walk at across different terrain to plan their route.  They probably also know they won’t fluff a launch and waste time there!

A tip I picked up from last year was to use poles.  It is transformational!  The arms do some of the work and it takes some weight off the legs.

It was hot, plenty of water was needed.  James Whitmore placed water bottles around his planned route the day before.  Others met their supporters on their route to refill.  I started with 2l then refilled at streams using purifying tablets, although I was caught out at one dried up stream, but fortunately the farmer’s wife was gardening in the neighbouring house and the hose came to the rescue.  As forecast, the wind picked up, Rosie Darwood had her second piece of bad luck with the wind being too strong when she reached the top of Penyghent, cloud covered her first launch.

The only other pilot I met was in the final stretch, John Westall at the end of his mammoth run from the northern part of the course heading for a flight into the campsite.  I looked at this the day before and didn’t think there was a chance I’d make it across the field in front of the launch.  Walking towards Langcliffe, a couple of higher rated gliders made it over with room to spare, so I thought, why not give it a go, it saves the legs on the walk down and it is a flying competition.  Following Westy on his EN D at least gave me some information as to what the air was doing. I made it over and to the campsite, just about on time, when others didn’t clear the field earlier in the day, albeit slightly different conditions.  So feedback on the Phi Symphonia 2 (landing above!) is that it seems to be performing as it says, a super A (safety of an A but with the performance of a B).

Above is a map of the course with the waypoints marked on (the start is in the middle, the finish at the bottom).  I have crudely drawn on the routes of each of the tracks I’ve seen.  It highlights how different everyone’s tactics were to suit their fitness, interpretation of the weather and the land and their ambitions for the day.  Of the 28 waypoints, only two weren’t visited, which is quite remarkable given the spread of them and we were all competing in the same conditions.  The top five finishers are what I’d describe as “runners”.  They have phenomenal levels of fitness covering c80km.  I travelled 48km, of which I estimate c8km was in the air and shocked myself with a 7th position finish.  Looking at my tracklog, I was surprised that I was stationary for 2hrs 10mins of the 10hr 15mins, which is largely packing and unpacking for the four flights, a couple of launches needed a reset, waiting for a lull in the wind for a launch and a few breaks along the way.  Going for a flight certainly gives a welcome rest from walking.

The top three places went to:

  • Tom Hodgkin 697 points
  • Jacob Aubrey 685 points
  • James Whitmore 636 points

To put their scores into context of how far ahead these guys are, 6th-15th places scored 390-280 points.  Put the clouds on different hills and rain in different areas, it would change the positions a little, but some thermals would really shake up the leader board!  Full score sheet - https://www.xcflight.com/x-dales-23-challenge/x-dales-23-results/ 

Some of the photos from Ed’s collection are in this article, others are here https://aeros.smugmug.com/Flying-2023/X-Dales-23-992023/

From all, a massive thank you to Ed for organising.  It was a great competition and whilst everyone at some stage in the day wished the hills were a little closer, sat around the campsite with their feet off the ground regaling the stories, the Dales were forgiven!

sections.title: Safety
sections.content:
Clive Sury reports

The pilot in charge

The phrase that stuck in my head when learning this sport was ‘You’re the pilot’. Right from the start providing an unambiguous statement that the decision to fly was my responsibility.  I had to think for myself and not blindly accept instructions from others. Sound advice.  

However, even knowing this there were times when I had mishaps through accepting guidance from others.  After one time of this in Morocco when I ‘slope landed’ in rocks and spiky bushes having been guided to get close to the hill for lift I finally realised that it was for me to decide what advice to follow - or not.  Shortly after this I was given feedback on another flight that I wouldn’t have had to go to land if I’d stuck closer into the hill.  But I knew what was right for me and was happy about the decision that I had made.  Finally, accepting my responsibility for when, where and how to fly.

This isn’t to say that I don’t want to be offered advice and take this on board.  Quite the opposite.  I’m grateful to anyone who gives the benefit of their experience and knowledge and would encourage others likewise.  One of the many great benefits of being in the club and flying with others is the knowledge shared.  I can’t think of one time whether flying or ground-handling that someone hasn’t been very supportive and I’ve learnt from.  However, I am conscious that it’s down to me, the pilot, to decide whether flying on a given day is within my experience and comfort zones.  Plus, to focus and gauge any flying instructions against what I can see and judge for myself.

I’ve written this piece to hopefully help others, notably recent CPs, equate advice with their own judgement and encourage them to think for themselves.  However, even experienced people can fall into the trap of ‘‘group think '’ and therefore, this message can have relevance for all of us.  Group discussion is certainly invaluable but ultimately, it’s the individual pilot that makes their own decisions.

Sometimes this is easy.  For example, last year on a trip I caught Covid on the way out through Manchester Airport (Covid was confirmed on a test when back in the UK).  One morning getting ready I fainted in my room.  Therefore, I wasn’t fit to fly that morning but I decided to be off the whole day.  Could I have flown in the afternoon when I was feeling better?  Maybe, but my reasoning was why risk it and just enjoy watching and learning from others instead.  And be happy with that.

Safe flying,

Clive.

P.S. - I’ll buy a drink at the Club social for the first person that posts on the Dales WhatsApp and Telegram group when the Dales Club Reserve Repack day is next year.
